what_it_does: Documents a design and plan for unifying operator surfaces around one shared craft vocabulary of material tokens, components, ordinal/icon/mono tiers, and resolver-driven composition.
observations: Frames 13 adversarial blockers as one underlying issue: thin parallel surface vocabulary instead of the shared craft vocabulary used by reference screens.
Defines the unification as a composable operator-surface system spanning material, component, ordinal, icon, mono, and composition layers.
Calls out existing `.dv-*` and `.ar-*` classes plus narrow near-white tokens as drift from the richer `.p-*` vocabulary.
Names specific shared components and motifs: `p-band`, `p-plate`, `p-inset`, `p-rows`, `p-rail`, `p-hatch`, dark mass, hatch texture, ordinal ramps, typed geometry, mono data tiers, and V-mark.
Plans to replace decision-card finish classes with shared `.p-*` components and tokens rather than surface-specific reimplementation.
Registers shape devices and duo connector as reusable DNA organisms.
Includes an unresolved fork between deepening tokens globally and scoping the change first to the operator surface.
Reconciles the plan with a RESOLVER frame where one invariant definition resolves against orthogonal axes through rule computation.
Identifies hand-written media/container breakpoints as cases that should be replaced by computed allocation from a device-root variable.
States a sequence: finish merge-sa keystone, prove the device axis on merge-sa, then generalize to other axes and pilot UI.
standouts: The document treats visual defects, component drift, and responsive behavior as expressions of one shared-system problem.
The resolver section shifts the plan from component reuse alone to rule-derived surfaces across device, viewer, mode, perspective, type, intent, resolution, state, posture, and register axes.
The status section positions current keystone fixes as the first increment of a larger unification cycle.
conventions: Uses numbered lifecycle sections: identify, design, plan, feedback, build, verify, check, improve, cycle.
Uses emphatic uppercase terms for architectural primitives and responsibilities, including UNIFICATION, RESOLVER, DEVICE AXIS, and DNA.
Uses dense internal vocabulary and references to named screens, roles, docs, and critique artifacts.
Uses arrows, middle dots, parentheticals, and bold emphasis to encode sequencing and conceptual relationships.
Contains no code symbols; it is a planning/design document rather than implementation.
concerns: Several referenced artifacts and terms are not defined inside the file, including the 13 blockers, scr-home, scr-piece-live, gen3-shapes, merge-sa, Contract B, and RESOLVER-BUILD.md.
The exact token names, component APIs, and resolver contract are described conceptually but not specified in executable or checklist-level detail.
The global-versus-scoped token fork is explicitly unsettled.
The resolver reconciliation section combines many axes and ownership lanes in a compact passage, which may make implementation boundaries hard to extract from this file alone.
notes: A design-plan memo that defines operator-surface unification as shared craft vocabulary plus resolver-computed allocation, with merge-sa as the stated proving path.
questions: What are the 13 adversarial blockers referenced by this file?
Which material/dark-polarity token fork is intended: global deepening or operator-surface scope first?
What concrete contract should `renderArchetype`, `renderExplained`, and `resolve(invariant, coordinate)` expose?
What measurements or visual criteria determine that the rebuilt surfaces pass verification?
Which parts of the resolver axes are owned by DNA, composition, projection, and fork in implementation terms?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the content: it identifies a unifying primitive, designs its vocabulary, outlines a build/verification cycle, and reconciles that plan with the resolver frame.
apparent_themes: shared vocabulary over parallel surface vocabularies
intention_signals: It appears intended to turn critique findings into a single system-level design direction, then guide implementation away from one-off card fixes toward shared vocabulary and resolver-derived surfaces.
novelty: The file combines visual design vocabulary, component architecture, and responsive-resolution theory into one operator-surface planning frame.
contribution: Contributes a neutral design substrate for fusing operator-surface craft vocabulary with resolver-based allocation: shared material tokens, `.p-*` components, ordinal/icon/mono tiers, reusable shape devices, and a rule-computed device-axis path.
summary_for_embedding: This is a design and planning memo for unifying operator surfaces through shared material tokens, `.p-*` components, ordinal/icon/mono tiers, reusable shape devices, and resolver-computed allocation.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to convert adversarial critique into a single unification plan and sequence the move from decision-card fixes to a reusable operator-surface system.
